name: Laguna del Campillo and Parque Regional del Sureste – circular from Rivas Este
name_original: Laguna del Campillo y Parque Regional del Sureste – circular desde Rivas Este
region_intro: Hiking around Rivas-Vaciamadrid offers diverse natural landscapes, with a significant portion of the municipality covered by the Parque Regional del Sureste. This area features rich river and lake ecosystems, including the protected wetland of Laguna del Campillo. Geological formations like the Cortados de Rivas and wooded areas along the Jarama and Manzanares rivers provide varied terrain for outdoor exploration. Best hiking trails around Rivas-Vaciamadrid The most popular hiking route is Laguna del Campillo and Parque Regional del Sureste – circular from Rivas Este, a 9.7 miles (15.5 km) trail that takes 4 hours 7 minutes to complete. This moderate route explores the protected wetland and regional park. Another top favourite among local hikers is Rivas Cliffs – Soto de las Juntas Lagoon loop from Rivas Vaciamadrid, an easy 3.7 miles (6.0 km) path. This trail offers views of the dramatic Rivas Cliffs and passes by the Soto de las Juntas Lagoon. Local hikers also love the Campillo Lagoon – Laguna del Campillo loop from Rivas Vaciamadrid, a 3.1 miles (4.9 km) trail leading through the Campillo Lagoon area, often completed in about 1 hour 16 minutes. Hiking around Rivas-Vaciamadrid is defined by river ecosystems, geological cliffs, and protected wetlands. The network of routes offers options for various ability levels, from easy strolls to more challenging moderate hikes. The routes in Rivas-Vaciamadrid are highly rated by the komoot community with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 90 reviews. More than 800 hikers have used komoot to explore Rivas-Vaciamadrid's varied terrain.
region_faq:
Q: How many hiking trails are there in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: Rivas-Vaciamadrid offers a wide selection of hiking opportunities, with over 50 distinct trails available. These routes cater to various skill levels, including easy strolls and more challenging moderate hikes.

Q: What kind of terrain can I expect on hikes in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: The terrain in Rivas-Vaciamadrid is quite diverse, characterized by rich river and lake ecosystems, dramatic geological formations like the &lt;strong&gt;Cortados de Rivas&lt;/strong&gt;, and wooded areas along the Jarama and Manzanares rivers. You'll find paths through protected wetlands, along riverbanks, and amidst pine forests.

Q: Are there any easy hiking trails suitable for beginners or families?
A: Yes, Rivas-Vaciamadrid has plenty of easy trails perfect for beginners and families. For example, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/40319823" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campillo Lagoon – Laguna del Campillo loop from Rivas Vaciamadrid&lt;/a&gt; is an easy 4.9 km (3.1 miles) route that takes about 1 hour 16 minutes. Another great option is the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/3963770" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rivas Cliffs – Soto de las Juntas Lagoon loop from Rivas Vaciamadrid&lt;/a&gt;, an easy 6.0 km (3.7 miles) path offering scenic views.

Q: Can I bring my dog on the hiking trails in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: Many trails in Rivas-Vaciamadrid, especially within the Parque Regional del Sureste, are generally dog-friendly. However, it's always recommended to keep your dog on a leash, especially in protected areas or near wildlife, and to check specific trail regulations before you go.

Q: What natural attractions or landmarks can I see while hiking?
A: Hiking in Rivas-Vaciamadrid offers access to several notable natural attractions. You can explore the protected wetland of &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2620629" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Campillo Lagoon&lt;/a&gt;, witness the dramatic &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/4232941" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Rivas Cliffs&lt;/a&gt;, or enjoy the serene environment of Soto de las Juntas at the confluence of the Jarama and Manzanares rivers. The &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/5123913" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Jarama Cliffs&lt;/a&gt; are another impressive geological feature.

Q: Are there good viewpoints along the trails in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: Yes, several trails offer excellent viewpoints. The Cerro del Telégrafo Park, for instance, provides spectacular views from its various lookout points. You can also find a notable &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/highlight/2864570" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;View of the confluence of the Jarama and Manzanares rivers from the Cortados de Rivas&lt;/a&gt;, offering a panoramic perspective of the landscape.

Q: What is the best time of year to go hiking in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: Rivas-Vaciamadrid is suitable for hiking year-round, but spring and autumn generally offer the most pleasant conditions with milder temperatures and vibrant natural scenery. Summer can be hot, making early morning or late afternoon hikes preferable, while winter offers cooler, crisp air for exploration.

Q: Is there parking available near the trailheads?
A: Yes, many popular trailheads and natural areas in Rivas-Vaciamadrid, especially those within the Parque Regional del Sureste and near lagoons, typically have designated parking areas. It's advisable to check specific route details for parking information.

Q: How can I reach the hiking trails using public transport?
A: Rivas-Vaciamadrid is well-connected to Madrid by metro (Line 9) and bus services, making it accessible for hikers relying on public transport. Many trails, particularly those starting from Rivas Este or Rivas Vaciamadrid, are within reasonable distance of public transport stops.

Q: What do other hikers say about the trails in Rivas-Vaciamadrid?
A: The hiking trails in Rivas-Vaciamadrid are highly rated by the komoot community, with an average score of 4.4 stars from over 90 reviews. Hikers often praise the diverse natural landscapes, the tranquility of the protected areas, and the well-maintained paths that offer a green escape close to the city.

Q: Are there any moderate hiking options for more experienced hikers?
A: Absolutely. For those seeking a more extended adventure, the &lt;a href="https://www.komoot.com/smarttour/e957796052" target="_blank" rel="noopener"&gt;Laguna del Campillo and Parque Regional del Sureste – circular from Rivas Este&lt;/a&gt; is a moderate 15.5 km (9.7 miles) trail that takes approximately 4 hours 7 minutes to complete, exploring the protected wetland and regional park.

Q: Are there any specific natural reserves or parks I should explore?
A: A significant portion of Rivas-Vaciamadrid is covered by the expansive &lt;strong&gt;Parque Regional del Sureste&lt;/strong&gt;, a biodiversity hotspot and a Special Conservation Zone. Within this park, the &lt;strong&gt;Laguna del Campillo&lt;/strong&gt; is a protected wetland teeming with wildlife, offering peaceful walking routes. You can also explore Parque Forestal Mazalmadrit for tranquil walks.

Q: Can I find trails that follow rivers or offer waterside views?
A: Yes, Rivas-Vaciamadrid is surrounded by the Jarama and Manzanares rivers, and many trails run along their banks. The terrain along the Jarama, in particular, features eroded canyons and gullies that create engaging paths. The Soto de las Juntas, at the confluence of these rivers, is a serene wooded area perfect for waterside walks.
tips: The Campillo geodesic vertex (671 m) is the highest point of the route, with extraordinary views of the Jarama River plain and the Velilla de San Antonio wetlands.

The plant cover that adorns the steep earthy slopes of the Jarama Valley is an exhibition of ingenious botanical adaptations to survive in the gypsum aljezares characteristic of the fertile plains of southeastern Madrid. The esparto grass forms large clumps of long, smooth leaves with a very dense and closed stump that retains moisture inside. The austere jabuna has a woody bearing, the stems are very fine and it does not exhibit bright colors that require high energy consumption, except in the flowering period. Another common plant of the cuts is the ontina or mugwort, used in popular medicine as an antidiabetic, vermifuge and invigorating, it develops thorns and discreet colors to avoid excessive perspiration. One of the most striking plants is the reseda, which has long, twisted stems. There are also thyme, scaly rosemary and harmaga, which defend themselves from the xerophytic environment with a reduced foliar surface and scaly leaves.

In February 1937, the Battle of Jarama took place in the surroundings of the current regional park, one of the hardest war events of the Civil War in the surroundings of Madrid. In the cuts of plaster and marl of the cliffs there are remains of military posts, fortifications and trenches used by the Republican troops to defend themselves from the national army that tried to cut off the supply of weapons and food from the Levant.

The Southeast Regional Park covers an area of 31,550 hectares in 16 municipalities of Madrid populations in constant expansion in the southeast of the capital, accentuating the peri-urban nature of the protected space, located in one of the most populated and industrialized areas of the Community of Madrid . A rich natural heritage of lagoons, wetlands, riverside forests, river groves and steppe hills make up a valuable set of ecosystems that are home to a unique flora and fauna in an environment of housing estates, roads, industrial farms and railways. A succession of strong contrasts that contribute to further elevate the environmental quality of the territory and the need to conserve these valuable wildlife refuges on the outskirts of the big city.

The El Campillo lagoon fits between the Rivas cliffs and the Jarama riverbed and is one of the 123 most important wetlands in the park. It is considered a Natural Reserve Zone and a Sensitive Ecological Zone, which is why its naturalization has been promoted, smoothing slopes, forming banks, creating islets and birdlife observatories, etc.
